segments.text: Hello everyone. I'm Greg Lemons, a senior developer with SymmetryUS.
And in the next 15 minutes, I want to show a simple idea with three concrete examples.
Web hooks by themselves are just signals. The real value shows up when that signal starts the next useful action, automatically inside the Autodesk workflows people are already using.
So we're going to stay close to real integration patterns here. Two examples are going to come from manufacturing with Fusion Manage.
And one is going to come from Forma in the AEC space.
In every case, the pattern is the same, receive an event, gather context, let AI reason over the context, and then write the result back into the system of record.
So a lot of systems notify us when something changes. The part is that part's not the hard part anymore.
The hard part is everything people have to do after the Hiller arrives.
Someone has to read it, gather related context, summarize what matters, and decide what to create or update next.
The opportunity for AI and integrations is not just better text generation, it is reducing the manual gap between an event and the next business action.
This is what all three of these examples are about, not another notification, but a started outcome.
Before we do jump in straight into demos, this is a reusable pattern. And I want you to keep that in mind.
First, an event, an APS webhook or data management event tells us that something meaningful happened.
Second, the integration loads the surrounding context, also using Autodesk platform services, gets related records, metadata attachments, document content, whatever the model needs.
Third, AI produces a useful decision artifact and assessment of summary or a draft action.
Fourth, the integration writes that output back into Autodesk. That right back is the difference between an interesting experiment and real workflow automation.
So it's going to be three workflows, one pattern. Here's the roadmap. First, we're going to show a change impact analysis in a fusion manage involved integration.
Second, in that same integration, we'll show a release note generation when an item reaches release.
And third, we'll move to Forma, where a newly uploaded document can become a draft RFI.
Now I'm going to walk through the examples using Autodesk platform service APIs and Forgeflow, which is our integration platform.
So we're going to roll right into demo one. And this is what we should look for as we express this demo.
We're going to have a webhook firing. We're going to load that context. We're going to have AI make a decision or assess a situation. And we're going to write back to that record.
So before we get into this first demo, I want to give a quick overview of the platform that will be demonstrating the integration processes on today.

This is Forgeflow. This is our integration platform. It's designed to follow that pattern that we just talked about all events around the left hand side. Those are triggers coming from different systems.
We've got Vault transition triggers. We've got webhooks coming from Fusion Manage. And then to the right are all of the action nodes that complete those processes.
So inside these triggers, we can actually create them inside the integration platform where we can create webhooks themselves.
And then a lot of dynamic nodes and kind of features that allow us to be very flexible with the processes that are built in here.
So the orange ones are generally Fusion Manage. The yellow ones are generally Vault in this case. And this is going to be the kind of platform that will show the examples on today.
So for this first demo, we're going to want to look for these things that we talked about in that pattern, a webhook fires, an event, a remote event.
